emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address.   *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

body.content:
[https://products.office.com/en-us/CMSImages/Office365Logo_Orange.png?version=b8d100a9-0a8b-8e6a-88e1-ef488fee0470]
Your message to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com couldn't be delivered.
sarah.jenkins wasn't found at sockcop.onmicrosoft.com.
admin   Office 365      sarah.jenkins
Action Required                 Recipient
Unknown To address

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Retype the entire recipient address before selecting Send.

If the problem continues, forward this message to your email admin. If you're an email admin, refer to the More Info for Email Admins section below.

Was this helpful? Send feedback to Microsoft<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=525921>.
________________________________

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

results.query: Based on the body content of the Passkeys email, when will Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication retire?
results.ground_truth_answer: According to the email body, Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.
results.app_answer:
Based on the email titled **"Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication"** received on July 22, 2026, the retirement details are as follows:

*   **Retirement Date:** February 1, 2027
*   **Action Required:** Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication (Passkeys) as the default experience, and users are encouraged to transition before the retirement of SMS and voice options.

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

admin   Office 365      sarah.jenkins
Action Required                 Recipient
Unknown To address

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Retype the entire recipient address before selecting Send.

If the problem continues, forward this message to your email admin. If you're an email admin, refer to the More Info for Email Admins section below.

Was this helpful? Send feedback to Microsoft<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=525921>.
________________________________

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

Th

body.content:
[https://products.office.com/en-us/CMSImages/Office365Logo_Orange.png?version=b8d100a9-0a8b-8e6a-88e1-ef488fee0470]
Your message to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com couldn't be delivered.
sarah.jenkins wasn't found at sockcop.onmicrosoft.com.
admin   Office 365      sarah.jenkins
Action Required                 Recipient
Unknown To address

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Retype the entire recipient address before selecting Send.

If the problem continues, forward this message to your email admin. If you're an email admin, refer to the More Info for Email Admins section below.

Was this helpful? Send feedback to Microsoft<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=525921>.
________________________________

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

results.query: Summarize the key action items from my inbox: the undeliverable email and the passkeys notice, and recommend next steps.
results.ground_truth_answer: 1) 'Undeliverable: Q4 Resource Allocation' (July 22, 2026): the email to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com bounced with error 550 5.1.10 because the recipient wasn't found - action: verify the correct email address and resend. 2) 'Passkeys by default...' (July 22, 2026, unread): Microsoft-provided SMS/voice authentication retires February 1, 2027 - action: migrate to passkey-based authentication before that date.
results.app_answer:
Based on your inbox, here is the summary of the key action items and recommended next steps regarding the undeliverable email and the passkeys notification.

### **1. Undeliverable Email: Q4 Resource Allocation**
*   **Issue:** Your email sent to `sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com` was not delivered because the recipient was not found in the directory.
*   **Key Details:** The system reported "Recipient Unknown," suggesting the email address is either misspelled or the account does not exist.
*   **Recommended Next Steps:**
    *   **Verify Address:** Check the Global Address List (GAL) or contact Sarah through another channel to confirm her correct email address.
    *   **Resend:** Once the address is verified, open the Non-Delivery Report (NDR) in Outlook and select **Send Again** to correct the recipient field.

### **2. Passkeys Notice: Authentication Retirement**
*   **Issue:** Microsoft has announced that SMS and voice-based authentication will be retired on **February 1, 2027**. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience for Microsoft Entra ID.
*   **Key Details:** This is a tenant-wide change requiring a shift toward phishing-resistant authentication.
*   **Recommended Next Steps:**
    *   **Assessment:** Review current user authentication methods in the Entra ID admin center to see how many users still rely on SMS or voice.
    *   **Planning:** Develop a migration strategy to transition users to passkeys or the Microsoft Authenticator app well before the 2027 deadline to ensure security compliance.

### **Summary Table of Priorities**

| Action Item | Deadline | Impact | Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resolve Undeliverable Email | Immediate | Communication Gap | High |
| Plan Passkeys Migration | Feb 1, 2027 | Security Compliance | Medium |
| Renew App Credentials* | Immediate | Service Interruption | High |

*\*Note: Your inbox also contains an urgent alert regarding an AzureAD app client secret (App ID 7868d053...) that is nearing expiration, which may require your immediate attention to prevent service downtime.*
